architecture.summary: The paper measures how interpretable features (extracted via sparse autoencoders from full-precision language models) degrade under quantization. A frozen SAE trained on full-precision activations serves as a fixed basis; the authors encode activations from both full-precision and quantized (INT8–INT4) versions of the same model on identical tokens, then measure per-feature survival via Pearson correlation. The mechanism is observational: no new model or training is introduced, only measurement of feature persistence across precision levels.

biblio.title: How Quantization Changes Interpretable Features: A Sparse Autoencoder Analysis of Language Models

claims.statement: 62.4 percent of active features surviving at INT6 on Pythia-70M
51.3 percent surviving at INT6 on Gemma-2-2B
feature survival is graded: features degrade systematically rather than failing all at once, with most non-survivors blurred rather than destroyed
survival is predictable from full-precision statistics alone, with cross-validated AUCs of 0.92 to 0.97
peak activation as the strongest marginal predictor
on Gemma-2-2B, INT7 improves perplexity while degrading 18.7 percent of features
quantization and matched-perplexity magnitude pruning damage strongly overlapping feature sets, with Jaccard overlap of 0.79 to 0.86
damage-score Spearman correlation of 0.98 between quantization and matched-perplexity magnitude pruning
